lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers repeatedly supplying sporting and athletic goods other than apparel and footwear, including fitness, golf, fishing, playground, protective, skating, skiing, racquet, team-sport, aquatic, and related equipment to external brands, retailers, institutions, distributors, or direct customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Apparel and footwear makers, bicycle and boat manufacturers, firearms and ammunition makers, pure retailers or wholesalers, brand licensors without US manufacturing operations, captive workshops, shells, inactive entities, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the normalized EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Manufacturers sell products and replacement items through wholesale, institutional, brand, retailer, and direct channels. Revenue is order and unit based, with repeat seasonal assortments, replenishment, replacement, and program purchases rather than subscriptions.

a.bridge: The 2002 Economic Census directly shows 41,416 production workers among 62,457 employees in NAICS 339920, while 2023 BLS data measure the broader miscellaneous-manufacturing occupation mix, where production is 48.81%, office and administrative support 11.06%, sales 4.34%, and design 2.06%. I use both to assign exposure to product and graphic design, demand forecasting, sourcing, compliance documentation, marketing, customer service, order processing, and planning while excluding most fabrication, assembly, testing, material handling, and equipment maintenance. The old exact-industry mix may overstate today's domestic production share after outsourcing, biasing exposure downward; the broader current mix may misstate this industry's brand and design intensity in either direction.
a.rationale: AI can accelerate product concepts and variants, technical and marketing content, assortment planning, demand forecasts, customer support, quality-document review, and administrative work. Physical prototyping, materials engineering, tooling, assembly, performance testing, finishing, and warehouse operations remain substantial.
a.change_evidence: Obtain current NAICS 339920 payroll by detailed occupation and conduct wage-weighted time-and-task studies across design, engineering, compliance, merchandising, sales, sourcing, production planning, factory operations, testing, and service at fifteen manufacturers across product segments.
caveats: The exact-industry workforce data are from 2002 and predate substantial outsourcing and ecommerce change.
Current BLS occupation data pool highly heterogeneous miscellaneous manufacturers.
AI exposure varies sharply between branded design-heavy goods and commodity fabricated equipment.

rho.bridge: Anthropic measures automation patterns and context constraints in enterprise API traffic, not sporting-goods manufacturers. BLS injury data and the industry's protective, playground, exercise, and load-bearing product scope indicate that design and compliance outputs often require physical testing and accountable review. I translate generic digital automation into implementation for design support, content, forecasts, and administration, then reduce for fragmented product data, seasonal change, safety validation, supplier integration, and physical prototyping. API users are more digitally mature than typical LMM manufacturers, biasing the unadjusted automation rate upward.
rho.rationale: Digitized CAD, ecommerce content, order histories, supplier data, and product catalogs support deployment within five years. Human review remains central where fit, biomechanics, load, impact, durability, material, and child or athlete safety must be validated in the physical world.
rho.change_evidence: Collect workflow-level AI adoption, approval, error, recall, test-failure, forecast, and labor-hour data from fifteen manufacturers and their PLM, CAD, ERP, ecommerce, quality, and customer-service systems over twelve months.
caveats: Anthropic API use is not a representative manufacturing sample.
BLS injury incidence concerns worker safety, not product-validation burden or AI adoption.
Implementation varies by digital maturity and product safety criticality.

e.rationale: The frozen count already targets the LMM band, and genuine sporting-goods manufacturers normally sell repeatedly to external brands, retailers, distributors, institutions, or consumers. Eligibility falls for pure importers, retailers, wholesalers, licensors, captive units, adjacent apparel, bicycle, boat, firearm or toy businesses, inactive firms, and companies whose normalized EBITDA is outside the band after seasonal and inventory adjustments.
e.change_evidence: Audit all 220 candidates using Census classifications, D&B/Hoovers, industry directories, customs and facility records, websites, ownership, domestic production evidence, customer channels, and trailing five-year normalized EBITDA.
caveats: This is a bounded judgment rather than an observed firm-level audit.
Brand owners and importers may be classified as manufacturers despite limited domestic production.
Seasonality, pandemic-era demand, inventory corrections, and channel concentration complicate EBITDA normalization.

s5.bridge: Census measures age among 4.1 million responding owners of US employer businesses in 2018, not qualifying transfers among eligible sporting-goods manufacturers. I use the 51% age-55-or-older share as succession pressure and discount it for five-year timing, family or management succession, continued ownership, strategic transactions, closure, and founder-dependent brands or product expertise. Niche manufacturers may have older founders than all employers, biasing the proxy downward, while age alone materially overstates external-sale probability.
s5.rationale: The historically fragmented industry supports a succession pipeline, but transferability requires stable channels, defensible products, supplier continuity, quality systems, and management beyond the founder. Many small lifestyle brands can close, license, or sell assets without a qualifying operating-company transfer.
s5.change_evidence: Match verified firms to ten years of PitchBook, GF Data, Capital IQ, Axial, industry transaction announcements, and state records, then survey owners on age, succession plans, sale intent, timing, and dependence on founder relationships.
caveats: The owner-age proxy is all-industry, response-based, and has data year 2018.
Historic fragmentation does not establish current transfer flow.
Owner age is not sale intent and founder dependence can reduce transferability.

q.rationale: Differentiated products, proprietary performance, brands, rapid launches, and direct channels can retain design, forecasting, service, and overhead gains. Retailer bargaining, seasonal line reviews, private label, imports, transparent ecommerce prices, and access to common design tools share benefits quickly for commodity goods. Safety and warranty savings are more retainable when they reduce recalls and returns.
q.change_evidence: Review five years of SKU- and customer-level prices, contracts, channel discounts, rebates, returns, warranty and recall costs, labor standards, and gross margins at twenty manufacturers segmented by branded, private-label, institutional, and direct sales.
caveats: No source directly measures retention of AI-created benefit.
Pricing power differs greatly across product categories and channels.
Retail concentration and imports can force productivity gains into lower wholesale prices.

d5.bridge: BEA real PCE covers other sporting and recreational goods from domestic and foreign suppliers and is broader than the current US NAICS 339920 manufacturing basket. It increased from $307.629 billion in 2021 to $347.273 billion in 2024. I translate the recent real consumption growth into moderate five-year product demand, then temper it for post-pandemic normalization, imports, used equipment, category mix, and the exclusion of some goods from NAICS 339920. The broad consumer series overstates domestic-manufacturer demand when imports gain share and may understate institutional or playground purchases.
d5.rationale: Health, recreation, outdoor participation, youth and school sports, home fitness, and replacement support demand, while discretionary spending cycles, product fads, used goods, post-pandemic normalization, and imports create downside. Recent real spending supports growth but not extrapolation of the full recent pace.
d5.change_evidence: Combine BEA PCE with Census shipments, USITC imports, SFIA participation and wholesale data, school and municipal procurement, retailer sell-through, and manufacturer orders by product; deflate and adjust for quality and channel mix.
caveats: The BEA category is broader than NAICS 339920 and includes imported goods.
Pandemic and reopening effects may inflate recent growth.
Demand differs across fitness, golf, fishing, playground, protective, winter, and team-sport categories.

o.rationale: Sporting equipment is physical and normally requires accountable design, material selection, fabrication, assembly, performance testing, and quality assurance; software alone cannot supply the current basket. Domestic LMM manufacturers can nevertheless be displaced by imports, retailer private label, contract production, self-fabricated simple goods, digital substitutes for some recreation, and shifts toward used equipment.
o.change_evidence: Measure domestic-versus-imported units, private-label sourcing, direct factory relationships, used-equipment substitution, digital-recreation substitution, and lost bids using customs, retailer, marketplace, warranty, and manufacturer data by product segment.
caveats: No direct source measures the future share requiring the current US operator type.
Imports can replace the lens operator without reducing end-user demand.
Operator need differs between complex safety-rated equipment and simple accessories.

narrative.executive_view: Sporting-goods manufacturing offers a credible AI layer in product development, merchandising, forecasting, customer service, and administration, but remains anchored in physical production and testing. Demand has grown in real terms, while import exposure, retailer power, product heterogeneity, and discretionary cycles limit visibility and benefit retention.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can accelerate concepts, product variants, technical and marketing content, demand planning, sourcing analysis, compliance-document review, customer support, and order processing. Physical engineering, prototypes, athlete or user testing, materials, tooling, assembly, durability, impact and load tests, and final quality release remain human and equipment intensive.
narrative.value_capture: Branded, proprietary, direct, or high-performance manufacturers can convert efficiency into faster launches, better availability, fewer returns, and margin. Private-label and commodity suppliers face seasonal bids, retailer concentration, transparent prices, common tools, and imports, so they are likely to share much of pure labor savings.
narrative.firm_availability: The frozen population implies a meaningful candidate pool, but pure importers, brand licensors, retailers, and adjacent-product companies can contaminate classification. Transferability also depends on channel durability, product liability, supplier control, working capital, and whether innovation resides beyond the founder.
narrative.demand_durability: Real spending on sporting and recreational goods rose through 2024, supporting continued demand from fitness, outdoor recreation, school and youth sports, and replacement. Post-pandemic normalization, fads, discretionary sensitivity, used goods, and imports warrant a broad range rather than extrapolation.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The NAICS code mixes very different products and safety profiles, and direct evidence on AI adoption, transfers, and retained benefit is thin. Product failures, recalls, seasonality, tariffs, inventory corrections, customer concentration, and supplier dependence can overwhelm workflow savings.
narrative.principal_driver: Rich product, order, and channel data can improve design-to-market speed and planning across a repeat product business with growing end demand.
narrative.principal_weakness: Import exposure and retailer bargaining can divert efficiency gains away from domestic manufacturers, while heterogeneous product cycles make demand difficult to underwrite.

sources.title: Sporting and Athletic Goods Manufacturing: 2002
sources.supports: Reports 2,158 companies, 2,233 establishments, 62,457 employees, and 41,416 production workers, with product classes spanning fishing, golf, playground, gymnasium and exercise, and other sporting goods.

sources.title: Miscellaneous Manufacturing - May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates
sources.supports: Broader current occupation mix reports production at 48.81%, office and administrative support at 11.06%, sales at 4.34%, and art and design at 2.06% of employment.

sources.title: Anthropic Economic Index report: Uneven geographic and enterprise AI adoption
sources.supports: Reports 77% automation patterns in first-party API business usage and identifies organized contextual information and data modernization as constraints on sophisticated enterprise deployment.

sources.title: Real Personal Consumption Expenditures: Other Sporting and Recreational Goods
sources.supports: BEA annual real PCE via FRED increased from $307.629 billion chained 2017 dollars in 2021 to $347.273 billion in 2024, with intermediate values of $307.690 billion in 2022 and $326.195 billion in 2023.

sources.title: Business Owners' Ages
sources.supports: Census 2019 Annual Business Survey graphic for data year 2018 shows 51% of responding employer-business owners age 55 or older, 43% age 35 to 54, and 6% age 34 or younger.

sources.title: Incidence Rates of Nonfatal Occupational Injuries and Illnesses by Industry, 2024
sources.supports: Reports a total recordable case incidence rate of 2.8 per 100 full-time workers for sporting and athletic goods manufacturing in 2024, supporting the persistence of physical operations and safety controls.

sources.title: Miscellaneous Manufacturing: NAICS 339
sources.supports: Explains that sporting and athletic goods use varied manufacturing processes and that processes for tennis racquets, golf balls, and other miscellaneous products differ significantly, supporting implementation heterogeneity.
